questions.question_text: How many statutory zones are covered by California's Natural Hazard Disclosure Statement?
questions.explanation: The California NHD Statement covers exactly 6 statutory zones: Special Flood Hazard Area, Dam Inundation Zone, Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone, State Fire Responsibility Area, Earthquake Fault Zone (Alquist-Priolo), and Seismic Hazard Zone.

questions.question_text: A home sits within a FEMA-designated Special Flood Hazard Area. Which NHD zone does this trigger?
distractors: Dam inundation zone
Seismic Hazard Zone
State Fire Responsibility Area
questions.explanation: FEMA-designated flood plains correspond to NHD Zone 1: Special Flood Hazard Area. This is distinct from a dam inundation zone, which relates to dam failure rather than direct flooding.

questions.question_text: A property is located near an active fault line mapped under the Alquist-Priolo Act. Which NHD zone applies?
distractors: Seismic Hazard Zone (liquefaction/landslide)
Dam Inundation Zone
Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone
questions.explanation: The Earthquake Fault Zone (Zone 5) corresponds to areas mapped under the Alquist-Priolo Earthquake Fault Zone Act, which prohibits most structures for human occupancy directly on active fault traces.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks why two different NHD zones — Earthquake Fault Zone and Seismic Hazard Zone — exist. How do they differ?
questions.correct_answer: Earthquake Fault Zone addresses fault rupture; Seismic Hazard Zone covers liquefaction and landslide
distractors: They cover the same risks but are administered by different agencies
Earthquake Fault Zone is federal; Seismic Hazard Zone is state-administered
They differ only in the insurance premiums they trigger
questions.explanation: Zone 5 (Earthquake Fault Zone) targets properties near active fault traces where surface rupture could occur. Zone 6 (Seismic Hazard Zone) covers areas at risk of liquefaction or earthquake-triggered landslides — ground failure rather than fault rupture.

questions.question_text: A seller hires a third-party NHD company to prepare the Natural Hazard Disclosure report. The company's report incorrectly omits a dam inundation zone. Who bears primary liability for the error?
questions.correct_answer: The NHD company, which assumes liability when retained for this purpose
distractors: The seller, regardless of who prepared the report
The listing agent, as the licensed professional of record
No one, because third-party reports are advisory only
questions.explanation: When a third-party NHD company is retained to prepare the disclosure, it assumes liability for errors in the report. This is one of the key benefits sellers gain from using reputable NHD services rather than completing the form themselves.

questions.question_text: A new subdivision is funded through a Community Facilities District (CFD) to pay for roads and utilities. What must the seller disclose to a buyer?
questions.correct_answer: The existence and approximate amount of Mello-Roos special taxes
distractors: Nothing; infrastructure costs are already reflected in the listing price
Only that a CFD exists but not the tax amount
The CFD only if it results in annual taxes exceeding $1,000
questions.explanation: Mello-Roos Community Facilities Districts levy special taxes on top of regular property taxes to fund new infrastructure. Sellers must disclose the existence of Mello-Roos taxes and their approximate amount.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchases a home and later learns they owe $4,000 per year in Mello-Roos taxes not disclosed during the sale. The buyer's best claim against the seller is based on:
questions.correct_answer: Failure to disclose a required supplemental tax obligation
distractors: Breach of the implied warranty of habitability
Violation of Proposition 13 tax limitation provisions
Misrepresentation of the property's appraised value
questions.explanation: California requires sellers to disclose Mello-Roos special taxes and supplemental tax obligations. Failure to disclose a $4,000 annual Mello-Roos assessment constitutes a failure to meet a mandatory disclosure obligation.

questions.question_text: A seller knows the home was built in 1965 and has lead-based paint. The buyer is informed through the federal lead paint disclosure but the seller says nothing further. Is this sufficient?
questions.correct_answer: No, California may require additional disclosure about lead paint hazards
distractors: Yes, federal lead paint disclosure satisfies all California requirements
Yes, if the buyer signs the federal form and waives further disclosure
No, the seller must also provide a lead paint inspection report
questions.explanation: California imposes environmental disclosure requirements beyond the federal lead paint disclosure. California may require additional disclosure and education materials related to lead hazards, especially for pre-1978 properties.

questions.question_text: Which of the following safety upgrades must a California seller typically disclose or certify compliance with before close of escrow?
questions.correct_answer: Smoke detectors, CO detectors, and water heater bracing
distractors: HVAC filter replacement and gutter cleaning
Roof certification and foundation waterproofing
Electrical panel upgrades and GFCI outlet installation
questions.explanation: California requires sellers to disclose and often certify compliance with smoke detector requirements (SB 969), carbon monoxide detector requirements, and water heater bracing requirements for seismic safety. These are among the mandatory safety disclosures.

questions.question_text: An older California home was built in 1955. What earthquake-related disclosure guide is the seller required to provide buyers?
questions.correct_answer: The residential earthquake hazards report for pre-1960 homes
distractors: The Seismic Hazard Zone map from the state geologist
The homeowner's earthquake insurance options guide
No earthquake guide is required unless the home is in a fault zone
questions.explanation: California requires sellers of pre-1960 residential buildings to provide buyers with a guide on residential earthquake hazards. This booklet covers common vulnerabilities in older construction such as cripple walls and unreinforced masonry.

questions.question_text: A seller's property is located in a Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone. What is the seller's disclosure obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the zone on the NHD Statement
distractors: None; fire hazard zones are disclosed through the NHD only for State Fire Responsibility Areas
Disclose only if the property lacks fire-resistant landscaping
Disclose only if the local fire department has issued a notice
questions.explanation: A Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone is one of the 6 statutory NHD zones. When a property falls within this zone, it must be disclosed on the Natural Hazard Disclosure Statement.

questions.question_text: A property is adjacent to a former military base where ordnance may still be buried. What is the seller's disclosure responsibility?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the military ordnance location as a known material fact
distractors: None; military land is exempt from California disclosure laws
Disclose only if an official government notice has been issued
Disclose only in the NHD section, not in the TDS
questions.explanation: California requires disclosure of known military ordnance locations as an environmental/safety hazard. If the seller has knowledge of proximity to a former military ordnance site, this must be disclosed as a material fact.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the difference between a State Fire Responsibility Area and a Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone in California's NHD?
questions.correct_answer: State FRA designates state responsibility for fire protection; VHFHSZ designates extreme fire risk level
distractors: They are the same zone described under different agency names
State FRA applies only to rural areas; VHFHSZ applies only to urban areas
State FRA is federally designated; VHFHSZ is locally designated
questions.explanation: The State Fire Responsibility Area (Zone 4) indicates where the state — not a local government — is responsible for fire prevention and suppression. The Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone (Zone 3) is a risk classification based on fire behavior modeling, which can include urban areas.

questions.question_text: A seller discovers a pest report reveals termite damage to the subfloor. California law requires:
questions.correct_answer: Seller to disclose the pest report findings as a material fact
distractors: Seller to repair all termite damage before close of escrow
Seller to pay for fumigation but not structural repairs
No disclosure if the damage is limited to accessible areas
questions.explanation: Pest report findings are a material fact that must be disclosed to buyers. California does not automatically require sellers to repair termite damage, but the findings of any pest inspection must be disclosed.

questions.question_text: A Mello-Roos tax shows up as a line item on a supplemental tax bill sent 60 days after close of escrow. The seller never mentioned it. The buyer's claim is strongest under which theory?
questions.correct_answer: Violation of California's supplemental tax disclosure requirement
distractors: Breach of the purchase contract warranty provisions
Fraud in the inducement based on misrepresentation of property value
Violation of Proposition 13 tax caps
questions.explanation: California requires sellers to disclose both Mello-Roos CFD taxes and the fact that supplemental tax bills will follow close of escrow. Failure to provide the supplemental tax notice is a specific statutory disclosure violation.
